text: The surgeon dissects the nerve along the left recurrent laryngeal nerve using a pre-esophageal approach, dividing the soft tissue between the esophagus and trachea. The left main bronchus is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon controls small vascular branches using robotic assistance, aiming to reach as high as possible on the right side. The thoracoscopic view reveals a visible knob, potentially the recurrent laryngeal or sympathetic nerve, as the dissection progresses towards the neck.
The surgeon identifies a potential thyroid gland in the surgical field and locates a bleeding vessel requiring attention.

The surgeon uses a robotic device to perform suturing on the thoracic wall, employing both bipolar and monopolar coagulation techniques simultaneously. The robotic arms are manipulated to suture and tie at two or three points on the chest wall.
Where is another point? This one? This one? Ok. Okay. How's it going? Okay.

The surgeon searches for the recurrent laryngeal nerve on the left side, using gauze for tissue manipulation instead of suction. The gauze is visible in the surgical field as the surgeon attempts to locate the nerve.

The surgeon uses the robotic third arm to dissect around the trachea, holding the left side of the trachea without affecting ventilation. A Maryland dissector is used to create space and divide tissue around the recurrent laryngeal nerve. The dissection progresses upwards, where the nerve is close to the trachea, requiring caution. Small vascular branches are controlled, and attempts are made to identify the recurrent laryngeal nerve and differentiate it from other nearby structures.

In the mid-position of dissection, the surgeon cuts nodal tissue to differentiate between anatomical structures. The recurrent laryngeal nerve is identified, running in a different direction from the symposium nerve. The surgeon notes the importance of preserving the left bronchial artery to avoid ischemia in the airway, while controlling lymph nodes near the pulmonary artery, which is visible with significant blood flow.
The surgeon aims to remove all lymph nodes surrounding the pulmonary artery and main bronchus, focusing on the anatomical structures in this area.

After removing the lymph nodes, the surgeon reveals preserved vessels and an intact knob without damage to the pulmonary artery. The surgeon proceeds to the upper area, holding the trachea, and performs node dissection along the left recurrent laryngeal nerve, carefully removing only the lymphatic tissue between the esophagus and the nerve to avoid damaging vascular supplies.
The surgeon uses an energy device to control small bleeding branches around the recurrent laryngeal nerve, carefully navigating the area to avoid damaging the nerve.

The surgeon carefully checks the distance between the energy device and the recurrent laryngeal nerve, maintaining a safe distance of three to five millimeters to avoid nerve damage. The nerve is visible close to the trachea in the upper neck area. The thyroid gland is identified, and the surgeon prepares to remove tissue below the thyroid, including the left cervical parasympathetic lymph node.

The surgeon removes additional lymphatic and fatty tissue in the lower neck, controlling vascular branches during the process. The dissection reveals nodes divided into two stations, 2L and 4L, according to the Asian system. The finalized view shows the aortic arch, recurrent laryngeal nerve, sympathetic nerve, and structures near the left hilum. The thyroid gland is visible, with surrounding tissue and the recurrent laryngeal nerve dissected.
